title: Was King Saul Impaled on the Wall of Beth Shean?
author: Amihai Mazar
allowedUseNotes: Copyrighted magazine article, paywalled. Summaries in project's own words only, drawn from secondary quotations found in web research, not the full paywalled text.
summary: Mazar's own popular-level synthesis addressing exactly the historical question this project's Beth-shan scene raises: whether the excavated record corroborates 1 Samuel 31's account of Saul's body displayed on the city wall. Reports (per secondary quotations located during this project's queue-#16 verification pass, 2026-07-16) that no fortification wall of any period has been identified at Tel Beth-Shean; that the relevant post-Egyptian-garrison Iron I stratum shows no distinctive Philistine material culture (no Philistine painted pottery); and that Mazar's renewed excavations reattribute the twin-temple complex Alan Rowe assigned to 'Level V' (and identified with the temples of Ashtaroth/Dagon named in 1 Sam 31:10/1 Chr 10:10) to an earlier stratigraphic horizon (Mazar's 'Late Level VI' / Stratum S-2), a phase that predates Rowe's own correlation of the temples with Saul's death. Mazar's stated conclusion is that the biblical account 'at best' reflects a Philistine military campaign into the valley rather than a garrison or lasting occupation.
confidenceNotes: TO VERIFY against primary copy: this project accessed the article's substance only through secondary quotations (blog posts and reader summaries quoting the piece, the BAS library's abstract/table-of-contents listing) during web research, not the paywalled original text itself. The specific findings recorded above (no fortification wall of any period; no Philistine material culture in the relevant stratum; the twin-temple stratigraphic reattribution to Late VI/S-2) were each corroborated across more than one independent secondary source quoting or paraphrasing Mazar directly, which is why this project treats them as citable, but full-article page-level verification has not been done.
